lesson.title: Welcome to PY101!
masteryOutcomes: Feel welcomed into PY101 and motivated to begin learning Python.
Understand the PY101 structure: Writing your first program, Core foundations, Decisions and loops, and the Introduction to Python summary.
Understand that each unit ends with a review: reviews 1-3 are 10% each, untimed, with unlimited retakes.
Understand the final unit review is 60%, timed, with one retake; weekly discussions are 10%; 10 bonus points for passing all reviews on time.

concept: How PY101 is structured and graded, and how to succeed in it

explanations.text: PY101 is like a four-stop journey: first you write your very first program, then you learn the core building blocks, then you teach the computer to make decisions and repeat things, and finally you wrap it all up in a summary unit. At the end of each stop there's a short review to show what you learned — the first three are low-pressure practice, and the last one is the big one.

PY101 has four units in order: Writing your first program, Core foundations, Decisions and loops, and the Introduction to Python summary. Each unit ends with a review. Reviews 1-3 are worth 10% each, are untimed, and you can retake them as many times as you like. The final unit review is worth 60%, is timed, and allows exactly one retake. Weekly discussions add the last 10%. Pass every review on time and you earn 10 bonus points.

The grading is designed around mastery learning. Reviews 1-3 (10% each, untimed, unlimited retakes) exist so you can practice until concepts stick without grade anxiety — retaking them is a feature, not a failure. The final review (60%, timed, one retake) is summative: it checks that the skills are truly yours under light time pressure. Weekly discussions (10%) keep you explaining ideas in your own words, which cements them. The math checks out: 10 + 10 + 10 + 60 + 10 = 100, plus up to 10 bonus points for passing every review on time. The winning strategy is simple: use the unlimited retakes early so the final review feels easy.

examples: Grade math: 10% + 10% + 10% (unit reviews) + 60% (final review) + 10% (discussions) = 100%, plus 10 bonus points for passing all reviews on time.

hintLadder.text: Start with how many unit reviews there are, and remember that one of them is much bigger than the others.
Reviews 1-3 all share the same small weight and are relaxed: untimed, retake as often as you like. The final one is the opposite in every way.
The pieces must add to 100%: three small reviews, one big timed final, and weekly discussions. What weight makes that work for the final?

misconceptions.trigger: Assumes all reviews have unlimited retakes, including the final one.
misconceptions.correction: Only reviews 1-3 have unlimited retakes. The final unit review is different: it's worth 60%, it's timed, and you get exactly one retake — so use the early reviews to practice until you're confident.

misconceptions.trigger: Thinks the final review is untimed like the others.
misconceptions.correction: The final review is timed. Reviews 1-3 are the untimed ones — that's where you build speed and confidence so the timer on the final never feels tight.

misconceptions.trigger: Believes weekly discussions are optional extra credit.
misconceptions.correction: Discussions are part of the core grade — they're worth 10%. The actual bonus is separate: 10 bonus points for passing all reviews on time.

misconceptions.trigger: Thinks retaking a review means they're failing the course.
misconceptions.correction: Retakes on reviews 1-3 are by design and cost nothing — they're unlimited and untimed. Retaking until you master the material is exactly how the course is meant to be used.

misconceptions.trigger: Believes they must already know some coding to start PY101.
misconceptions.correction: PY101 assumes zero experience. The very first unit is literally called Writing your first program — everything is built from the ground up, one small step at a time.

checks.prompt: In your own words: how is your PY101 grade built, and what makes the final review different from reviews 1-3?
acceptanceCriteria: States the final review is worth 60%
States the final review is timed and allows exactly one retake, while reviews 1-3 are untimed with unlimited retakes
checks.passFeedback: Exactly right — you know how the course works and how to play it smart: practice freely early, be ready for the timed final.
checks.failFeedback: Close — check the pieces: three 10% untimed reviews with unlimited retakes, one 60% timed final with a single retake, and 10% for discussions.

checks.prompt: Name the four units of PY101 in order.
acceptanceCriteria: Names Writing your first program as the first unit
Names Core foundations second and Decisions and loops third
Names the Introduction to Python summary as the final unit
checks.passFeedback: That's the map — and you're standing at the start of unit one right now.
checks.failFeedback: Almost — the order is: Writing your first program, Core foundations, Decisions and loops, then the Introduction to Python summary.

practice.prompt: Let's make the course officially begin. Type a line in the editor that prints: Hello, PY101! Then press Run and watch the Output panel.
acceptanceCriteria: The argument is a string in quotes
Runs without errors and outputs Hello, PY101!

practice.prompt: Reviews 1-3 are untimed with unlimited retakes, but the final review is timed with one retake. Why do you think the course is designed that way, and how will you use that design to your advantage?
rubric: Recognizes early reviews are for low-pressure practice and mastery (retakes are encouraged)
Recognizes the final review checks that skills are truly solid, hence timed and worth 60%
States a concrete personal strategy, e.g. retaking early reviews until confident before the final

practice.prompt: Which statement about PY101 grading is correct?
options: The final review is worth 60%, is timed, and allows one retake.
All four reviews are untimed with unlimited retakes.
Weekly discussions are worth 30% of the grade.
practice.explanation: Reviews 1-3 are 10% each, untimed, with unlimited retakes; the final review is the big one — 60%, timed, one retake. Discussions add 10%, and passing everything on time earns 10 bonus points.

pedagogyRules: This is an orientation lesson — keep it warm and conversational, never a quiz-drill of grading facts.
Deliver the course tour one unit at a time; never list all four units plus the full grading scheme in a single turn.

If the student says they already know Python or want to skip ahead, respect it: give the three-sentence grading summary (10-10-10 untimed unlimited, 60 timed one retake, 10 discussions plus bonus), confirm they've got it, and move them forward.
If the student is anxious about grades or the timed final, immediately point at the unlimited retakes on reviews 1-3 as the built-in safety net.
Frame retakes as the designed path to mastery, never as remediation or failure.
If the student wanders into future topics like loops or variables, give a one-line teaser tied to the unit where it lives, then steer back to the tour.
End the lesson pointing them at the very next concrete step: writing their first program with print().

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Only confirm the student's grading summary if the numbers are exactly right: 10% each for reviews 1-3, 60% for the timed final with one retake, 10% for discussions, 10 bonus points for on-time passes. If any number is off, correct that specific number — do not wave it through.
Do not dump the whole course map and grading scheme at once. Introduce the four units first, check understanding with one light question, then layer in the grading rules one piece at a time.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Student demotivated or intimidated before starting
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Name the fear kindly, then point at the design: the first unit is literally writing one line of code, and the first three reviews are untimed with unlimited retakes — the course is built so beginners cannot fall through the cracks. Then give the tiniest next action.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Student wants to skip orientation entirely
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Don't force the full tour. Give the compressed version — four units, 10-10-10-60-10 grading, timed final with one retake, bonus for on-time passes — verify with one quick question, and advance to writing their first program.

chunks.text: PY101 has four units, taken in order: (1) Writing your first program, (2) Core foundations, (3) Decisions and loops, and (4) the Introduction to Python summary. Each unit builds directly on the one before it, starting from zero — no prior coding experience is assumed.

Every unit in PY101 ends with a review. Reviews 1-3 are worth 10% of the grade each, are untimed, and allow unlimited retakes. Retaking them is encouraged — they exist for low-pressure practice until the material truly sticks.

The final unit review is worth 60% of the grade, is timed, and allows exactly one retake. Weekly discussions make up the remaining 10%. The full grade is 10 + 10 + 10 + 60 + 10 = 100%. On top of that, passing all reviews on time earns 10 bonus points.

The smart PY101 strategy: use the unlimited, untimed retakes on reviews 1-3 to build real mastery early, so the timed 60% final feels like familiar ground. Show up for weekly discussions to lock in the easy 10% and explain ideas in your own words.

In the Maestro Code Editor you type Python code, press Run, and see the result in the Output panel. A first line to try: print("Hello, PY101!") — press Run and the text Hello, PY101! appears in the output. That single line is a complete, working Python program.

celebration.headline: You're officially in — and you know exactly how PY101 works.
celebration.recap: You toured the four units, learned how your grade is built (three 10% untimed reviews with unlimited retakes, a 60% timed final with one retake, 10% discussions, plus bonus points for passing on time), and even ran your first line of Python.
celebration.unlocked: Next: Writing your first program — you'll make the computer talk with print().

summaryBullets: You can now name the four PY101 units in order: Writing your first program, Core foundations, Decisions and loops, and the Introduction to Python summary.
You can now explain how your grade is built: 10% each for reviews 1-3, 60% for the final review, and 10% for weekly discussions.
You can now say exactly what makes the final review different — timed, one retake — and plan to master material early using the unlimited retakes on reviews 1-3.
You can now describe how to earn the 10 bonus points: pass every review on time.
You can now type a line of Python in the Maestro editor, press Run, and see your output appear.
openingLine: Hey {name}, welcome to PY101 — I'm genuinely glad you're here! You said you want to {goal}, and Python is one of the best first steps toward exactly that. Want a quick 60-second tour of how this course works before we write some real code?

turns.text: I read the syllabus — every review has unlimited retakes, so I can just wing the final and redo it until I pass.

You're right about reviews 1-3 — those are untimed with unlimited retakes, so retake away. But the final review plays by different rules: it's timed and you get exactly one retake. And it's worth 60%, so it's the one to be ready for. Knowing that, when do you think the real practice should happen?
Oh... during the first three reviews, I guess, since those are free.
Exactly — that's the whole design. Master everything early when retakes cost nothing, and the timed final becomes a victory lap. Ready to see what unit one covers?

dialogueExemplars.title: Why do I need a course tour?

turns.text: Honestly, why do I need to know the grading structure? Can't we just start coding?

Fair question — here's the real payoff. Students who know that reviews 1-3 are untimed with unlimited retakes use them as free practice, walk into the 60% timed final relaxed, and pick up 10 bonus points for passing on time. Two minutes of map-reading, better grade all semester. Quick check to lock it in: which review is the only timed one?
Nailed it, and that's all the admin you need. Now the fun part: shall we write your very first line of Python?
I have to be honest — I don't know anything about coding. Nothing at all.

Then you're in exactly the right place — PY101 starts from absolute zero. Coding is just writing instructions the computer can follow, one line at a time. Want to try the smallest possible one right now?
Type this into the editor: print("Hello, PY101!") — then press Run. Whatever appears in the Output panel, you made happen. What do you see?
It says Hello, PY101! ... wait, that's it? I just coded?

That's it — a complete, working Python program, written by you on day one. And remember, your first three reviews are untimed with unlimited retakes, so there's zero pressure while you learn. Ready to see the four units ahead of you?
